coredata.dc:description: Advancements in sustainable agriculture are crucial for enhancing crop germination and early growth, addressing significant economic losses stemming from poor soil health, unpredictable climate conditions, and the overuse of chemical inputs that degrade ecosystems and reduce crop yields. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial that functions as an artificial photosynthetic pigment, have shown significant promise in enhancing plant growth and photosynthesis. This study investigates the effects of CQDs on the growth and photosynthesis parameters (net assimilation, transpiration rate, stomatal conductance, water use efficiency and Rubisco activity) in hydroponically grown green mustard (Brassica juncea) plants. Various CQDs concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 400 ppm) were applied as foliar sprays. The application of CQDs foliar spray significantly enhanced the number of leaves, leaf surface area, plant height, and both fresh and dry biomass of green mustard plants. Additional parameters, including net assimilation rate, transpiration rate, stomatal conductance, and intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE) were also examined. The results demonstrated that treatment with 150 ppm CQDs significantly increased CO2 assimilation rate and Rubisco activity by 58 % and 33 %, respectively, compared to the control. Additionally, the treatment at 150 ppm had the highest iWUE, suggesting that CQDs at this concentration efficiently uses water during photosynthesis to achieve a high yield. Furthermore, the effects of CQDs on plants under white light and red/blue (R/B) light were studied respectively. The chlorophyll fluorescence (Fv/Fm), effective quantum yield (ΦPSII), and light response curve of the control, 150 ppm, and 400 ppm treated plants were measured across different wavelengths. The electron transfer mechanisms that take place in the CQDs/chloroplast complex are described in this work. This study shows that CQDs have significant potential for increasing global agricultural production in the future.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advancements in sustainable agriculture are crucial for enhancing crop germination and early growth, addressing significant economic losses stemming from poor soil health, unpredictable climate conditions, and the overuse of chemical inputs that degrade ecosystems and reduce crop yields. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial that functions as an artificial photosynthetic pigment, have shown significant promise in enhancing plant growth and photosynthesis. This study investigates the effects of CQDs on the growth and photosynthesis parameters (net assimilation, transpiration rate, stomatal conductance, water use efficiency and Rubisco activity) in hydroponically grown green mustard (Brassica juncea) plants. Various CQDs concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 400 ppm) were applied as foliar sprays. The application of CQDs foliar spray significantly enhanced the number of leaves, leaf surface area, plant height, and both fresh and dry biomass of green mustard plants. Additional parameters, including net assimilation rate, transpiration rate, stomatal conductance, and intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE) were also examined. The results demonstrated that treatment with 150 ppm CQDs significantly increased CO2 assimilation rate and Rubisco activity by 58 % and 33 %, respectively, compared to the control. Additionally, the treatment at 150 ppm had the highest iWUE, suggesting that CQDs at this concentration efficiently uses water during photosynthesis to achieve a high yield. Furthermore, the effects of CQDs on plants under white light and red/blue (R/B) light were studied respectively. The chlorophyll fluorescence (Fv/Fm), effective quantum yield (ΦPSII), and light response curve of the control, 150 ppm, and 400 ppm treated plants were measured across different wavelengths. The electron transfer mechanisms that take place in the CQDs/chloroplast complex are described in this work. This study shows that CQDs have significant potential for increasing global agricultural production in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing global population and the pressing need to ensure food security are key drivers of innovation in agriculture. As the world’s population continues to rise, food insecurity becomes an increasingly urgent challenge. According to the International Institute for Applied Systems Analysis, global food consumption is expected to surge by approximately 102 % from 2009 to 2050 [1]. Recent findings clearly indicate the necessity for technological advancements within the agricultural sector to tackle emerging challenges and promote sustainable growth.

Plant nano-bionics is a promising field that studies the interactions between nanoparticles (NPs) and the subcellular structure of plants in relation to nanotechnology and plant science. Engineered nanomaterials (NMs) present a potential solution for addressing future food supply challenges. In recent years, significant interest has focused on the application of NMs in agriculture field to boost plant growth and productivity due to its unique properties and effectiveness. Recent studies demonstrate that various NMs, including TiO2, ZnO, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), can significantly improve crop yields by enhancing nutrient uptake and stress tolerance [2,3]. Among these, CQDs are metal-free NMs and have been shown to promote agriculture yield [4].

CQDs, a zero-dimension (0D) NMs has emerged as a rising star due to its numerous advantages, including chemical inertness, excellent solubility, non-toxicity, high biocompatibility, and low production cost [5]. Recent extensive research on CQDs has revealed their potential to significantly enhance plant growth. Wang et al. [6] reported a 14.9 % increase in the fresh biomass of mung beans and a 17 % rise in the shoot dry biomass of wheat when treated with CQDs at 20 ppm. Swift et al. [7] further demonstrated that CQDs treatment led to a 17 % increase in shoot biomass, an 18 % rise in grain yield, and an 11 % enhancement in seed production in wheat. Similarly, our prior studies on maize and rice indicated a 4.8 % and 20.9 % increase in plant height, respectively [8]. Moreover, treatment with 150 ppm of CQDs resulted in a substantial rise in stomatal conductance, reaching up to 56 % in rice and 18 % in maize.

Plant growth and development are profoundly influenced by their response to light. CQDs have emerged as promising nanomaterials for enhancing plant growth and stress tolerance [9]. While red-blue (R/B) light combinations are commonly recommended to boost crop yield in indoor hydroponic systems [10], there is growing interest in using CQDs as photosynthesis enhancers to achieve similar outcomes. If CQDs enhance photosynthesis by absorbing UV light and converting it into longer, photosynthetically active wavelengths, white light may prove to be even more critical than R/B light. However, most research on CQDs has focused on their effects under white light, with limited exploration of how different light spectra impact CQDs behaviour in plants. CQDs-treated plants can exhibit altered photoluminescence properties, which can provide valuable insights into photosynthetic processes. Analyzing changes in photoluminescence, such as fluorescence, can reveal how CQDs affect a plant's ability to absorb light, convert energy, and produce organic compounds [11].

To fully exploit the potential of CQDs for enhancing plant growth, it is essential to understand their interactions under varied lighting conditions. This study aims to fill this gap by investigating the light response of CQDs across different lighting scenarios, offering new insights into their application in indoor hydroponics farming, focusing on green mustard, a nutrient-rich vegetable crop favoured for its ease of cultivation. The biocompatibility of CQDs has been previously evaluated using in vitro and in vivo models, demonstrating good cell viability in 3T3 fibroblast cells even at high concentrations (IC50 = 900 ppm) and minimal developmental toxicity in zebrafish embryos below 2000 ppm [8]. These findings indicate the safety of CQDs for potential agricultural applications, including use with Green Mustard leaves. By elucidating the interactions between CQDs and light response, this research seeks to provide valuable insights into optimizing hydroponic farming practices.

Biochar samples used in this study were received from Pakar Go Green Sdn. Bhd made from empty fruit bunches biochar (EFB) via a controlled pyrolysis process. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA, C3H8O, 98 %) was received from Merck. Deionized water was used throughout this study and all chemicals were used as received without further purification.

The comprehensive synthesis method for the CQDs was outlined in an earlier publication [8], using acid-free microwave assisted process. The biochar was mixed with the solvent and the mixture was heated in the conventional microwave at a low power setting for 5 min, followed by centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the CQDs/EFB biochar mixture was filtered and the supernatant liquid of CQDs was collected. For this study, the CQDs was diluted to concentrations of 50 ppm, 100 ppm, 150 ppm, 200 ppm, and 400 ppm. These varying concentrations were subsequently utilized in the physiological experiments to assess their effects on plant growth and development.

The morphology and particle size of the CQDs were examined using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2010) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For sample preparation, a small quantity of CQDs suspension was dispersed in isopropanol (IPA) and sonicated for 5 min to ensure uniform dispersion. Subsequently, a drop of the solution was deposited onto copper grids coated with an amorphous carbon film. CQDs were characterized using attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy in the range of 400–4000 cm−1, utilizing a Thermo Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

Green mustard (Brassica juncea) seeds, branded as Green World, were sourced from Syahirah Sdn. Bhd. Initially, the seeds were soaked in distilled water to select the best seeds, which were then sterilized with 75 % ethanol. The seeds were germinated in cocopeat, kept in dark conditions for two days. After germination, seedlings were transferred to a hydroponic system equipped with a Samsung LED white grow light (18 W, 240 V, 50/60 Hz). The system's temperature was maintained between 27–30 °C with a humidity level of 46 % to ensure optimal growth conditions. AB fertilizer was added into the water tank of the hydroponic system once a week and its electrical conductivity was maintained at 1.8 EC.

The plants were treated with five CQDs concentrations: 0, 100, 150, 200, and 400 ppm. CQDs was applied via foliar spraying on the second and third weeks, with 1.5 ml and 2.5 ml used per plant, respectively, to account for the increase in leaf size. Control plants were sprayed with distilled water. Each treatment was replicated nine times. After six weeks, the plants were harvested. The most effective CQDs concentration was determined based on the photosynthetic rate and overall plant growth. Selected concentrations of 0 ppm, 150 ppm, and 400 ppm were then used in a subsequent experiment where plants were exposed to two lighting conditions: white light (Samsung LED grow light, 18 W, 240 V, 50/60 Hz) and a combination of red and blue light (Philips, 18 W, 220 V, 50 Hz). Light response curve data were collected on Days 3 and 7 for all treated plants. The hydroponic system provided a controlled environment with optimal light, temperature, humidity, and nutrient availability for green mustard growth. The AB fertilizer supplied essential macro- and micronutrients, while CQDs enhanced nutrient uptake, photosynthesis, and stress tolerance.

The morphological characteristics of mature green mustard plants were evaluated after six weeks of growth by assessing the number of leaves, leaf surface area, plant height, and both fresh and dry biomass. Each treatment group comprised nine plants, with the results reported as average values. Leaf count was conducted manually across all replicates. The dimensions of the fifth leaf, previously labeled for consistency, were measured using a ruler. Plant height was also measured with a ruler, positioning the '0′ mark at the cocopeat surface. Fresh biomass was determined immediately post-harvest by cutting the plants at the base of the stem and weighing them using a mass balance. Following this, the plants were dried overnight in an oven, and the dry biomass was subsequently recorded.

A portable LICOR fluorometer photosynthesis system (LI-6800; Li-Cor Inc., Lincoln, NE, USA) equipped with an integrated fluorescence leaf chamber (6800-01A) was utilized to measure key photosynthetic parameters, including net assimilation (A), transpiration rate (T), and stomatal conductance (gsw). The CO2 concentration within the leaf chamber was maintained at 400 µmol mol−1, and the photosynthetic photon flux density (PPFD) was set to 1500 µmol m−2 s−1. Measurements were taken once leaf photosynthetic parameters had stabilized, typically requiring 15 to 25 min. The intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE) of the plants was calculated using Equation (1) [12]:
iWUE = CO2 Assimilation rate (A) / Stomatal conductance (gsw)…. (1)

Additionally, the fluorometer was employed to determine light response curve parameters, including chlorophyll fluorescence (Fv/Fm), effective quantum yield (PhiPSII), and photosynthesis rate. These measurements were conducted on Days 3 and 7 following the CQDs foliar application.

The ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (Rubisco) activity was quantified using a CUSABIO Rubisco assay kit, procured from Wuhan Huamei Biotech Co. Ltd. Reagents, stock solutions, and samples were prepared in strict accordance with the manufacturer's protocol. All experimental procedures were executed as outlined in the kit manual. Optical density measurements were taken within 5 min for each well, employing a BioTek Synergy HTX microplate reader set to wavelengths of 450 nm and either 540 nm or 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light response effects on plant growth under CQDs treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized concentrations of CQDs were selected. The plants were treated with CQDs via foliar application. These plants were subsequently exposed to two different light wavelengths: white light and a red/blue (R/B) light combination. The white light was provided by a Samsung LED grow light (18 W, 240 V, 50/60 Hz), while the R/B light was from Philips (18 W, 220 V, 50 Hz). Light response curve data were collected on Days 3 and 7 post-CQDs application to assess the effects on photosynthetic activity.

The chloroplasts were extracted from 8 weeks green mustard leaves during the vegetative stage using the Sigma Chloroplast Isolation Kit (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis MO, USA). Freshly harvested leaves of green mustard were used throughout the experiment and all experiment steps were performed at 2 to 4 °C. Before isolating the chloroplast, the plants of green mustard were kept in the dark one day before for optimal yield of intact chloroplast and avoiding high levels of starch accumulation. All the chloroplast suspensions were kept in the dark or on ice until further use. The detailed experiment steps for isolating chloroplasts were conducted in accordance with the previous publication [8].

Chlorophyll fluorescence using PL spectroscopy was conducted to elucidate the electron transfer mechanism between CQDs and chloroplasts. Initially, the green mustard seeds were washed with deionized water three times to remove any residue exists. And then the seeds were transferred to a seedling tray for germination. CQDs solution (150 ppm) was sprayed onto the leaves of green mustard and kept in the dark one day before the isolation of chloroplasts. In the control experiments, green mustard plants without any application of CQDs were set as control samples. The isolation of chloroplasts for both the control group and CQDs-treated groups were prepared using the same procedures as mentioned in Section 2.9. Intact chloroplasts were brought for PL analysis on the same day.

All experimental data were subjected to statistical analysis. Significant differences between treatments were evaluated using one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s HSD and Duncan's multiple range test at a significance level of 0.05. Each analysis was conducted in triplicate. Symbols in the statistical figures represent different rankings, with 'a' denoting the highest yield, followed sequentially by 'b,' and so forth. Identical symbols across different concentrations indicate no significant difference among those groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and Functional Properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM images of the as-prepared CQDs are presented in Fig. 1 a. The CQDs are predominantly spherical in shape, monodispersed, and exhibit uniform size distribution within the 1–6 nm. The high-magnification HRTEM image (inset) further confirms the crystalline nature of the CQDs, showing distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is characteristic of the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. The FTIR spectrum of CQDs shown in Fig. 1b exhibited a broad O–H stretching vibration around 3290 cm−1, along with peaks corresponding to C–H at 2970 cm−1, C=O at 1641 cm−1, C=C at 1421 cm−1, and C–O at 1047 cm−1. These signals confirm the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, on the CQDs surface, which contributes to their high-water solubility [8]. Under daylight, the CQDs solution appears greyish, while under UV light (365 nm), it exhibits a bright blue-green fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CQDs on morphological growth parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different CQDs concentrations on key growth parameters, such as leaf number, leaf area, plant height, and biomass of green mustard plants are shown in Fig. 2 . Across all metrics, CQDs treatments showed varying effects, with some concentrations leading to significant improvements in plant growth. Fig. 2a showed that the number of leaves increased significantly with CQDs treatment, peaking at 100 and 150 ppm at 26.32 % and 28.82 %, where values were statistically different from the control (0 ppm), indicating the most robust leaf development at these concentrations. No significant differences were observed between 50, 200, and 400 ppm and the control. A similar pattern was observed in leaf area, as shown in Fig. 2b, whereby leaf area significantly increased by 29 % and 40.58 % at 100 to 150 ppm respectively compared to the control, with a notable reduction at 400 ppm. These findings are consistent with previous reports, where the application of CQDs significantly enhanced plant growth. For instance, CQDs treatment increased leaf area by 31 % in Romaine lettuce compared to control plants [13]. Similarly, in N. benthamiana, CQDs application resulted in a remarkable 57.3 % increase in leaf area relative to untreated controls [14]. This suggests that moderate concentrations of CQDs promote better leaf expansion, whereas higher concentrations may inhibit this effect.

Plant height showed an increase at concentrations of 100 ppm with increment of 34.56 % compared to the control respectively, as shown in Fig. 2c. A similar approach was employed to prepare a CQDs solution, which was subsequently applied to rice and maize plants in a previous study that showed a notable increase in plant height, with rice exhibiting a 4.8 % growth enhancement and maize showing a significant 20.9 % increase at 150 ppm [8]. These findings demonstrate that different plant species exhibit varying responses to CQDs treatments at different concentrations, despite the use of similar preparation methods and precursors. In another study, wheat plants were treated with cerium-doped CQDs at various concentrations, where treatment with 25 ppm resulted in a significant 46 % increase in plant height compared to control plants [15].

Next, both fresh and dry biomass increased with CQDs concentration, peaking at 100 to 150 ppm significantly of 177 % at the highest compared to control, while higher concentrations (200 to 400 ppm) showed no significant improvements between treatments and even indicated a decline, suggesting a potential inhibitory effect at the highest levels (Fig. 2d-e). The observed increase in plant biomass is supported by El-Saadony et al. [16], who suggested that CQDs treatment may alleviate the detrimental effects of environmental stressors on plant growth, allowing treated plants to allocate more resources towards growth, thereby resulting in enhanced biomass weight. In a previous study, CQDs application on mung beans resulted in a 14.9 % increase in fresh weight, while wheat plants exhibited a 17 % increase in shoot dry biomass [6,7]. Similarly, Rome lettuce treated with 30 ppm of CQDs showed a 48.1 % and 41.98 % increase in fresh and dry shoot weight, respectively, compared to the control [13]. Furthermore, lettuce treated with 5 ppm FR-CQDs achieved a 51.14 % yield increase over untreated plants [17]. Notably, N. benthamiana treated with 50 ppm CQDs displayed an impressive 247.03 % increase in fresh weight, underscoring the remarkable growth-promoting potential of CQDs.

The results indicate that CQDs have a concentration-dependent impact on green mustard plant growth, with moderate concentrations (100 to 150 ppm) yielding the most significant improvements across key physiological parameters, including leaf number, leaf area, plant height, and biomass accumulation. These findings suggest that CQDs may act as effective photosynthesis enhancers, likely by improving light absorption and energy conversion efficiency in the plants, leading to enhanced carbon assimilation and growth. At concentrations of 100 to 150 ppm, CQDs likely optimize photosynthetic activity, leading to improved chloroplast function and higher carbohydrate production, which directly impacts vegetative growth as reflected by increases in leaf number, area, and plant biomass. The improvement in fresh and dry biomass further supports the role of CQDs in enhancing nutrient absorption and utilization, which is crucial for robust plant growth. However, higher concentrations (200 to 400 ppm) appear to have diminishing or even inhibitory effects on plant growth, as seen in the reductions in leaf area and biomass at 400 ppm. This suggests that excessive CQDs may induce oxidative stress or disrupt nutrient transport processes, negating the beneficial effects observed at lower concentrations. This trend aligns with previous studies indicating that nanoparticles, including CQDs, can have biphasic effects, where low doses are stimulatory but higher doses become toxic [18]. The visual observations in Fig. 3 align with the quantitative data, showing enhanced growth of green mustard plants at 100 to 150 ppm CQDs, evidenced by larger and healthier foliage compared to the control and other treatments. Notably, plants exposed to 400 ppm exhibit reduced leaf size and overall growth, further supporting the conclusion that high concentrations may induce inhibitory effects on plant development.

This study highlights the potential of CQDs obtained from this work as a promising nanoparticle for enhancing plant growth and biomass production, particularly at concentrations around 100 to 150 ppm. However, care must be taken to avoid excessive application, as higher concentrations may lead to adverse effects, potentially due to overstimulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) production or disruption of metabolic balance. Future studies should focus on elucidating the underlying mechanisms of CQDs interaction with plant cellular processes and optimizing their use for agricultural applications. An overview of the impacts of CQDs on the growth of various plants in agriculture is summarized in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of CQDs on photosynthesis parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Aside from evaluating the plant's physiological traits, measuring the plant's photosynthetic parameters is crucial for demonstrating the efficiency of CQDs treatment. The photosynthesis parameters such as transpiration rate, net assimilation, stomatal conductance, intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE), and Rubisco activity at CQDs concentrations ranging from 0 to 400 ppm were studied in Fig. 4 . The results reveal significant differences across treatments for all measured parameters. Transpiration, the process by which water is absorbed by plant roots, transported through the plant, and evaporated from the aerial parts into the atmosphere, plays a pivotal role in the plant water cycle [24]. The transpiration rate showed a marked increase of 28 % at 100 ppm compared to the control, followed by decreased at 50, 150, and 200 and 400 ppm suggesting a possible inhibition of water vapor loss at this concentration (Fig. 4a). This result aligns with findings from Wang et al. [14] demonstrated that the application of CQDs at a concentration of 50 ppm significantly increased the transpiration rate of N. benthamiana by 0.15–1.47 % compared to control plants, indicating that CQDs can enhance plant physiological processes related to water loss and vapor exchange.

Net assimilation rate is a key parameter in plant physiology and agronomy, representing the rate at which a plant assimilates atmospheric carbon dioxide, while accounting for carbon losses due to respiration [25]. It serves as an indicator of the plant's efficiency in converting carbon dioxide into organic compounds, such as sugars, during photosynthesis, after subtracting the carbon dioxide produced during light respiration. The data are fundamental for assessing a plant's growth, overall health, and productivity, providing critical insights into its physiological performance and agronomic potential [26]. Net assimilation was significantly enhanced at all CQDs concentrations, highest at 150 ppm, with a 56 % increase compared to the control (Fig. 4b). In our earlier study, the application of developed CQDs at 150 ppm enhanced the net assimilation rate by 56 % in rice and 16 % in corn. Similarly, a separate research group demonstrated that CQDs treatment at 5 ppm increased the net assimilation in rice by 14.7 % [17].

Stomatal conductance measures how efficiently carbon dioxide enters and water vapor exits the leaf through the stomata, which are small pores on the leaf surface [27]. This process is vital for both photosynthesis and transpiration, directly affecting the plant's ability to regulate water use and support growth. Stomatal conductance plays a key role in balancing gas exchange, influencing overall plant health and productivity. Stomatal conductance was significantly higher at 100 ppm, showing an increase of 58 % from the control, followed by a gradual decline at higher concentrations, with the lowest conductance observed at 200 ppm (Fig. 4c). A study reported that the benth plant (N. Benthamiana) exposed to CQDs at 50 ppm had a 17.5 % to 110.8 % higher stomatal conductance than the control plant [14].

iWUE is defined as the ratio of net CO2 assimilation to stomatal conductance, serving as a key indicator of a plant's efficiency in utilizing water for carbon fixation during photosynthesis [28]. Elevated iWUE values reflect greater water-use efficiency, indicating that the plant is optimizing water resources to maximize carbon assimilation [29]. Fig. 4d showed iWUE exhibited a substantial increase at 150 ppm, which was 28 % higher than the control, considering how stomatal conductance was low despite increase in net assimilation rate at the concentration. This observation is highly significant, indicating that CQDs enhances photosynthetic efficiency without a corresponding increase in water loss through transpiration. The increase in photosynthetic performance suggests that CQDs improves the plant's light absorption and energy utilization, optimizing photosynthetic processes while maintaining water-use efficiency [18].

Next, the amount of rubisco is regulated to correspond to the leaf's capacity to create new ribulose bisphosphate (RuBP). Rubisco activity increased significantly at 100 and 150 ppm, with the highest levels recorded at 150 ppm at 33 % increase compared to control (Fig. 4e). Activity at 400 ppm decreased but remained higher than the control, indicating that CQDs enhance photosynthetic enzyme function at moderate concentrations. Several studies have reported similar increases in Rubisco activity [30]]. For example, Li et al. [31] demonstrated a 42 % enhancement in Rubisco activity in rice plants, reaching 1400 U mg−1 or moles min−1 per mg of enzyme. Additionally, Wang et al. [6] observed a 30.9 % increase in Rubisco activity in mung bean (200 U mg−1) compared to the control (150 U mg−1). Similarly, Zhang et al. [32] reported that CQDs exposure increased Rubisco activity in mung bean by approximately 20.5 % relative to the control. These studies further corroborate the role of CQDs in promoting Rubisco activity and improving photosynthetic performance across different plant systems.

The data from this study demonstrate that CQDs have a profound influence on several physiological and biochemical processes in green mustard plants. The enhancement of transpiration rate, net assimilation, and stomatal conductance at lower concentrations (50 to 150 ppm) suggests that CQDs stimulate gas exchange and photosynthetic activity, likely by improving the plants’ ability to absorb light and utilize it more efficiently for carbon fixation. This finding is in line with previous reports indicating that nanomaterials can act as photosynthesis enhancers by boosting electron transport and energy conversion efficiency [33]. Interestingly, at 150 ppm, the transpiration rate and stomatal conductance decreased, while both net assimilation and intrinsic water use efficiency peaked. This suggests that at optimal CQDs concentrations, plants may shift their physiological strategies, reducing water loss through transpiration and stomatal opening while maximizing carbon assimilation and photosynthetic efficiency. This phenomenon could be attributed to a more efficient rubisco enzyme function, as evidenced by the increased rubisco activity at this concentration, which allows for improved carbon fixation even under conditions of reduced stomatal conductance. The decline in performance at 200–400 ppm, including reduced net assimilation, stomatal conductance, and rubisco activity, suggests that high concentrations of CQDs may begin to exert toxic effects or trigger stress responses, potentially through the overproduction of ROS or disruption of metabolic pathways.

In conclusion, the results highlight those moderate concentrations of CQDs (100 to 150 ppm) provide the most significant benefits to green mustard plants by enhancing photosynthesis, water use efficiency, and enzyme activity, while higher concentrations may lead to negative effects. The precise mechanism underlying these effects likely involves improvements in both photosynthetic efficiency and stomatal regulation, driven by increased rubisco activity.

The overall enhancement of the photosynthesis process by CQDs in relation to the results obtained in sections 3.2 and 3.3 was shown in Fig. 5 . At the molecular level, CQDs serve a light-harvesting molecules by enhancing photon absorption, improving electron transfer efficiency, and increasing ATP and NADPH production. This could lead to more effective CO2 fixation and glucose production. When the CQDs was applied on the leaf, the CQDs absorbed excess photons from the light while emitting PAR photons. The chlorophyll actively absorbed the photons on the upper part of the leaf while the stomata are found in the lower part of the leaf. The Reaction Center (RC) captured the emitted photons from the CQDs, which eventually prolong the electron transfer chain (ETC). By prolong the ETC, more ATP and NADPH were produced. At the end of the Calvin cycle, glucose (starch), ADP and NAPH+ were produced. ADP and NAPH+ were utilised in light dependent reaction. The glucose end product will be used to support plant growth and development. The higher the concentration of ATP and NADPH, the longer the net assimilation (A) and Rubisco activity will last. This will promote the creation of greater glucose molecules, resulting in increased plant growth. In brief, CQDs treatment improved plant photosynthesis rate, resulting in increased net assimilation (A), stomatal conductance (gsw), transpiration (E), water usage efficiency (A/gsw), and Rubisco activity. As a result, plant growth parameters such as plant height, fresh weight, dry weight, number of leaves and leaf area increased in correlation with an increase in photosynthesis rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of white light and R/B light response on CQDs treated plant
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 illustrates the effects of white and R/B light on plants treated with 150 ppm CQDs. Under regular white light exposure, the plants appeared healthier and larger, with a weight increase of 16.1 % compared to 11.2 % under R/B light. While R/B light is known to promote flowering, this is often unnecessary for many vegetable plants, where the primary goal is to maximize leaf or root growth rather than fruit or flower production. Thus, under the current experimental conditions, 150 ppm CQDs treatment under normal light is a good alternative for better indoor hydroponics crop production.

Figs. 7 to 9
presents the effects of varying concentrations of CQDs on photosynthetic parameters in green mustard plants under white light and R/B conditions, evaluated on Day 3 and 7 in the indoor hydroponics system. White LED light encompasses the full light spectrum, while R/B light is limited to blue (400 to 500 nm) and red (620 to 750 nm) wavelengths [34]. The key parameters for this study were maximum quantum efficiency of PSII photochemistry (Fv/Fm), quantum yield of PSII (ΦPSII), and photosynthetic rate as a function of light intensity, specifically photosynthetic active radiation (PAR).

Fv/Fm, is the ratio of variable to maximum fluorescence after dark adaptation, which shows the plant's potential for photosynthetic activity and the PSII's maximum efficiency in converting light energy to chemical energy [35,36]. Fig. 7a demonstrates that under white light on Day 3, the Fv/Fm value at 150 ppm treatment was approximately 1.25 % lower, while the 400 ppm treatment exhibited an increase of around 1.23 % compared to the control. By Day 7, Fv/Fm values increased in all groups, with the 400 ppm treatment showing the highest at 3.75 % increased, suggesting enhanced photochemical efficiency in PSII over time. Higher Fv/Fm values at 400 ppm suggest that the plant's photosystem II (PSII)successfully captureda significant number of light photons. The graph showed that from Day 3 to Day 7, the Fv/Fm values increased by 1.3 %, 3.8 %, and 2.5 % for the control, 150 ppm, and 400 ppm treatments, respectively. The 150 ppm treatment exhibited the most improvement. This suggests that the initial decrease in Fv/Fm observed on Day 3 at 150 ppm may have been due to the plants' stress response to the nanomaterial application [37]. However, by Day 7, the plants appeared to have recovered and adapted to the stress, leading to enhanced photochemical efficiency. In response to oxidative stress induced by nanoparticles, the plants likely activated their antioxidant defences mechanisms and repair processes, contributing to the recovery in photosynthetic efficiency [2]. Additionally, direct interactions between the nanomaterials and the PSII complex may have further enhanced Fv/Fm, promoting more efficient energy conversion under white light condition.

In Fig. 7b, the plants Fv/Fm values exposed to R/B light were recorded. The graph showed the Fv/Fm values remained relatively stable across the different concentrations at Day 3, with a slight increase for plants treated with 150 ppm CQDs at 1.22 % compared to control, followed by 400 ppm, indicating improved PSII efficiency. By Day 7, the Fv/Fm values exhibited a decline of 0.24 %, 1.32 %, and 1.1 % for control, 150 ppm, and 400 ppm treatments, respectively. Notably, the 150 ppm treatment sustained the highest photochemical efficiency among all groups. The decrease in the Fv/Fm value of plants exposed to R/B LED light after 7 Days, particularly at varying concentrations of CQDs, attributed to several interrelated factors affecting photosynthetic efficiency and stress responses. Red light at 640 nm is primarily absorbed by chlorophyll, promoting growth, while blue light at 464 nm influences photomorphogenesis and regulates stomatal opening (Metallo et al., 2018). However, excessive exposure to high-intensity red or blue light can lead to photoinhibition, causing a decline in the Fv/Fm ratio over time. This proved by the study of Luo et al. (2024) that shown the lettuce plants exposed to higher ratios of blue light tend to have better photosynthetic performance initially, but prolonged exposure can lead to a decrease in Fv/Fm due to oxidative stress and damage to PSII. The interaction between CQDs and light quality can also influence the rate of photosynthesis. At high CQDs concentrations under R/B LED lighting, the accumulation of ROS might overwhelm the plant's antioxidant defenses, leading to a reduction in Fv/Fm values over time as shown at 400 ppm (Muneer et al., 2014). In summary, there were no significant differences between treatments, as the photochemical efficiency of the plants remained stable across all conditions, reflected by consistent Fv/Fm values, suggesting that the CQDs were not as effectively activated as they were under white light. This stability indicates the plants' resilience to the tested conditions, maintaining optimal photosynthetic function within the healthy Fv/Fm range of 0.74 to 0.85 (Tarakanov et al., 2022).

Next, ΦPSII is the term used to describe the photochemical energy conversion in PSII in a steady state (Contributors, 2020; [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 8, ΦPSII, which measures the actual quantum yield of PSII in light conditions, showed a significant difference across treatments at Day 3. The 400 ppm CQDs treatment exhibited a 13 % higher ΦPSII, and the 150 ppm treatment showed an 11.7 % increase compared to the control. However, by Day 7, the trend reversed, with the 150 ppm treatment showing a significantly higher ΦPSII at 6.7 % greater than the 400 ppm treatment, indicating a shift in photochemical efficiency over time (Fig. 8a). A higher ΦPSII value signifies a more efficient utilization of absorbed photons in photosynthesis, enhancing the conversion of light energy into chemical energy [39]. This boosts the ETC, ultimately generating more ATP and NADPH [40]. Additionally, a higher Fv/Fm value from Fig. 7a at 150 ppm CQDs reflects a more robust and efficient photosynthetic system, which correlates with increased ΦPSII, as more electrons are available to drive ATP and NADPH production [41].

The Fv/Fm result correlated to the ΦPSII values under R/B light both on Day 3 and Day 7, were generally showed no significant difference (Fig. 8b). The highest quantum yield of PSII was observed at 150 ppm on Day 7, indicating enhanced photochemical efficiency in response to CQDs after 7 days of application at 7.94 % compared to control. The 400 ppm group showed a slight reduction in ΦPSII by 2.94 % compared to 150 ppm suggesting that higher concentrations may begin to exert inhibitory effects on PSII performance over time. This suggests that CQDs may exert a subtle influence on enhancing PSII efficiency, despite the observed variability under R/B light.

From the findings in this section, the full light spectrum and R/B light has the Fv/Fm range from 0.8 to 0.82 which proved that there is no photoinhibition occur and maximum photons been captured. Higher Fv/Fm values suggest that the plant's photosystem II (PSII) was successful in capturing a significant number of light photons, roughly 80 % [42]. The remaining 20 % will be lost to the environment as heat. The values of Fv/Fm were in the range of around 0.8, which is close to 0.83 and indicates normal and healthy growth [43]. It can be seen that values on Day 7 were higher than Day 3, and that values for treated plants tended to be higher than control plants under both lights condition. However, the statistical analysis indicated no significant difference between treated and control plants. After a week, CQDs shows its properties and detected that Fv/Fm were slightly higher for full white light spectrum than R/B light. The CQDs may be interacting with one or more of other pigments in a way that enhances electron transfer in white light conditions, but not in R/B light conditions. This is because these wavelengths of light are most effective in activating the photosynthetic pigments in the plant chloroplast, which are responsible for capturing light energy and converting it into chemical energy. The results proved that photochemical efficiency of captured energy transferred to the reaction centre (RC) will be higher. This will eventually be led for the higher ΦPSII. However, there are huge difference noticed between white light spectrum and R/B light whereas plants exposed to white light spectrum had higher ΦPSII than R/B light condition. This demonstrates that CQDs actively transferring the electrons to the RC in full light spectrum rather than R/B light. Similar results were determined on Day 3 and 7. At Day 3, the ΦPSII of 150 ppm and 400 ppm treatments under the white light were 15 % and 19 % higher than R/B light, respectively. At Day 7, plants exposed to white light had improved their ΦPSII by 20 % with 150 ppm, and plants treated with 400 ppm shown a 13 % improvement. This shows that the CQDs effectively transfer the captured photons for the photochemical under the white light condition rather than the R/B light condition. In short, 150 ppm treatment of CQDs with the condition of white light is the best option for the higher plant growth. This demonstrates that the CQDs effectiveness is higher when the plant is exposed to full light spectrum rather than R/B light. CQDs effectively captured UV–vis and transferred most of the captured photons for the photochemical reaction in the full light spectrum compared to R/B light. This shows that even though the plant had received CQDs treatment a week earlier, the CQDs chlorophyll pigment still effectively increases energy conversion for the photochemical process under both lights’ conditions.

Light response curves are a valuable tool for elucidating the relationship between light intensity and photosynthesis in plants [44]. Fig. 9a–d illustrate that photosynthetic rates increased more rapidly with rising light intensity (PAR) in plants treated with 150 ppm and 400 ppm CQDs compared to the control, under both white and R/B light conditions. Under white light, the curves for both Day 3 and Day 7 show that plants treated with CQDs (150 ppm and 400 ppm) exhibit higher photosynthesis rates than the untreated plants (0 ppm) (Fig. 9a-b). The effect is more pronounced at higher light intensities, with plants treated with 400 ppm CQDs showing the highest photosynthesis rates. Under R/B light (Fig. 9c-d), the photosynthesis rate followed similar pattern as white light. However, the effect of CQDs under R/B light is even more significant than under white light, particularly at higher light intensities. Notably, the photosynthetic rate for control plant is higher under R/B (∼8 to 9 µm−2s−1) compared to white light (∼ 6 to 7 µm−2s−1) at different light intensities. The outcome emphasizes that R/B light have a higher quantum yield for CO2 assimilation (Liu et al., 2021) compared to white light. This is probably because plants under R/B light, which aligns more closely with the absorption spectrum of chlorophyll, enhancing overall photosynthetic efficiency. Under white light, 400 ppm showed higher photosynthetic rate compared to 150 ppm. However, under R/B light, 150 ppm showed higher photosynthetic rate compared to 400 ppm. The difference in behaviour may be tied to the different roles that CQDs play under different lighting conditions. The findings indicate that CQDs primarily acts as light converters under white light [21] but act as photosensitizers under R/B light (Jovanović et al., 2023).

White light consists of a broad range of wavelengths, including ultraviolet (UV), visible (blue, green, red), and infrared (IR) light, including those that may not be fully utilized by plant photosynthetic pigments. CQDs, as light converters, absorb less efficient wavelengths and re-emit light in the red and blue regions, which are more useful for photosynthesis. This enhanced light quality leads to increased photosynthetic efficiency. In plants, CQDs used as light converters transform less-efficient wavelengths (like green, UV, and IR) into wavelengths which are more efficiently absorbed by chlorophyll. This process helps plants optimize light utilization for photosynthesis. Essentially, light converters increase the amount of usable light by shifting parts of the spectrum to regions where plants have better absorption capacity. Under white light, 400 ppm showed higher photosynthetic rate compared to 150 ppm as there are more CQDs are available to convert less efficient wavelengths of white light into useful red and blue light. This increases the overall availability of optimal light for photosynthesis, resulting in a higher photosynthetic rate. Plus, the photosynthetic machinery is not saturated with light energy under white light as quickly as it is under R/B light because the full spectrum is not entirely useful for photosynthesis without CQDs helping to convert it.

Under R/B light, CQDs primarily act as photosensitizers, which means they absorb light in the red and blue wavelengths and efficiently transfer this energy to the plant’s photosynthetic machinery (e.g., chlorophyll). This leads to an enhanced rate of photosynthesis, as seen in the steep curves in Fig. 8c-d. Photosensitizers can also help generate ROS (Singh et al., 2018), which can further drive energy-dependent processes in the plant, thereby improving photosynthetic efficiency. Under controlled conditions, low levels of ROS can act as signalling molecules, promoting plant growth and stress responses. Since R/B light is already highly efficient for photosynthesis, CQDs mainly enhance energy transfer rather than converting light. At 150 ppm, the concentration of CQDs is ideal for enhancing energy transfer without oversaturating the system. This optimal level allows CQDs to boost photosynthesis by enhancing the absorption of light and improving the energy transfer to the plant's photosystems. At this concentration, the CQDs provide just the right amount of assistance to the natural photosynthetic process. At higher concentrations of 400 ppm, CQDs may begin to experience self-quenching, where they absorb light but are unable to effectively transfer that energy to the plant's photosynthetic apparatus. Instead, the energy may be dissipated as heat or non-radiative processes, reducing the efficiency of photosensitization. Plus, CQDs might scatter light instead of absorbing and transferring it effectively, reducing the amount of red and blue light that actually reaches the plant’s natural photosynthetic pigments. This phenomenon accounts for 150 ppm, which provides a balance between enhancing photosensitization and allowing optimal chlorophyll functionality under R/B light.

This appears to be a good explanation to the improved photosynthetic rate for CQDs treated plants at 150 ppm compared to 400 ppm under R/B light. Under white light, the constant exposure to higher energy wavelengths may reduce the light converting role of CQDs, akin to the deactivation of photocatalysts with time. On the other hand, under the lower energy wavelengths of R/B light, CQDs appear to maintain its function as a photosensitizer, as can be seen by the increased photosynthetic rate of the plants at Day 7. The outcome emphasizes that R/B light have a higher quantum yield for CO2 assimilation (Liu et al., 2021) compared to white light. Since the photosynthetic rate of control plant remains fairly constant, it is clear that the behaviour of treated plants is influenced by the light spectra it is exposed to.

The findings in Section 3.4 demonstrated that CQDs predominantly acts as light converters under white light and as photosensitisers under R/B light. The difference in behaviour could be attributed to the multiple roles that CQDs acts under different illumination conditions. Fig. 10 shows the proposed electron transfer mechanism by CQDs/chloroplast under white light and R/B light illumination. In the study, the CQDs play the role as a light converter under white light illumination, it actively converting the absorbed UV photons to PAR photons (400–700 nm). When CQDs absorb UV light, they emit light in the PAR range, effectively enhancing the amount of light available for plants to drive photosynthetic processes. By converting non-PAR UV light into PAR, CQDs significantly broaden the range of light that plants can utilize, allowing them to absorb more photons that contribute to photosynthesis, even if they are not in the initial PAR spectrum. This enhances the plant’s overall photosynthetic efficiency and growth potential [21,45,46]. This light-converting property makes CQDs an exciting area of research for agricultural and indoor farming technologies, as they could be integrated into lighting systems to improve plant growth under controlled conditions. Given that red and blue light are already ideal for photosynthesis, CQDs serve as energy transfer enhancers (photosensitisers) rather than changing light wavelengths (light-converter). Under R/B light, CQDs act as photosensitizers by absorbing photons at specific wavelengths and facilitating energy transfer, which leads to ROS formation via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Following light irradiation, the CQDs photosensitiser transitions from the ground state (single-linear state (S0)) to the excited single-linear state (S1). The photosensitizer's excited state is extremely unstable, and excess energy is lost by fluorescence emission. The excited single linear state can produce a more stable excited triple state (T1) via intersystem crossings, resulting in the formation of ROS and phosphorescence. CQDs facilitate the generation of ROS molecules, such as singlet oxygen (1O2) or superoxide anions (O2−), when energy is transferred to molecular oxygen. This happens through energy transfer from the excited CQDs to molecular oxygen (O2), a process facilitated by the triplet state of the CQDs [47]. The production of low levels of ROS in plants can be beneficial which can act as beneficial signalling molecules at controlled levels, promoting growth and activating stress responses in plants [48].

The results of this study align with the findings presented in a report by Produce Grower, which highlights the ongoing debate surrounding the use of white light versus R/B light combinations for optimizing crop yield. The report emphasizes that, although R/B light is often considered more effective for instantaneous photosynthesis, the studies reviewed suggest that lettuce grown under warm-white light exhibited significantly higher light use efficiency (LUE). Specifically, the LUE was 29 % lower under a R/B light ratio compared to white light, suggesting that white light may provide a greater advantage for crop yield in certain growing condition (Produce [49]. Study from Alrajhi et al. [50], mentioned that white light, including UV supplementation, provided the best overall yield and quality for lettuce crops compared to various R/B light combinations. The study indicated that white light resulted in the highest leaf area and fresh weight, suggesting its superiority in promoting growth and yield. These findings possess significant practical implications for controlled-environment agriculture, which includes vertical farming and greenhouse production, where optimising lighting conditions is vital for maximising crop yield. While there is substantial evidence supporting the beneficial effects of quantum dots on plant growth, direct long-term comparative studies focusing on crop yields under white light versus R/B light specifically in conjunction with quantum dot treatments are limited. This study demonstrated that the application of CQDs significantly enhanced plant growth, particularly in green mustard plant, by optimizing the concentrations and the light spectrum. The results indicated that plants treated with CQDs not only exhibited increased biomass accumulation but also showed superior growth performance under a white light spectrum compared to control groups and R/B light spectrum. This suggests that integrating CQDs can effectively improve photosynthetic efficiency and overall plant health when combined with broad-spectrum lighting.

Fig. 11 shows the extracted chlorophyll fluorescence spectra of 150 ppm CQDs, chloroplast and CQDs treated chloroplast at 250 nm excitation under white light illumination. It was found that the CQDs peak was detected at 300–400 nm, the chloroplast peak was detected at 650–780 nm, and the CQDs/chloroplast peak was detected in both ranges. Chloroplast from each green mustard plant treatment were isolated and fluorescence spectra were measured under 250 nm excitation wavelength. It is normal practice to measure the photoluminescence graph of carbon dots at an excitation wavelength of 250 nm. This is because it falls within the UV spectrum and has the potential to efficiently excite the carbon dots' electrons, resulting in the emission of visible light. The PL emission peaks of CQDs occur around 300 to 400 nm, indicating that CQDs exhibit strong absorption in the ultraviolet to near-visible light range. This wavelength range corresponds to the light absorption properties of chlorophyll, enabling CQDs that mimic chlorophyll's function and effectively enhance light capture for photosynthesis. The higher PL intensity observed for the 150 ppm CQDs treatment suggests that this concentration optimally enhances the CQDs’s light-harvesting properties, possibly due to an ideal quantum confinement effect or an optimal surface functionalization [51]. Similar findings have been reported in studies investigating CQDs as efficient light-harvesting materials. For instance, Li et al. [31] demonstrated that CQDs exhibit strong photoluminescence at specific concentrations, significantly enhancing light absorption and utilization in plant systems. Their study found that the optimal CQDs concentration increases photoluminescence intensity, enhancing photosynthetic efficiency due to the ability of CQDs to capture UV-light and convert it into photosynthetically active radiation. Similarly, Wang et al. [6] observed that CQDs with higher photoluminescence under specific wavelengths contributed to improved energy transfer to chloroplasts, promoting better plant growth and higher photosynthetic rates. It can be said that the chloroplast tends to emit electrons at higher states at wavelengths of 650–780 nm with the same level of PSII, but the CQDs prefers to emit electrons at wavelengths of 400–450 nm [52–54]. This proves that the CQDs is expected to have resonant energy transfer with chloroplasts acting as electron donors, resulting in a lesser fluorescence intensity than the pure CQDs. According to Chandra et al. [55], since electrons are transferred to the chloroplast, its energy increases, resulting in a greater fluorescence intensity peak than the pure chloroplast. CQDs acts as an electron donor, donating electrons to the chloroplast for continued photosynthesis. In this study, CQDs in chloroplasts serves as a light converter from UV radiation to PAR under white light illumination. Recent research has demonstrated that there is no energy resonance between the CQDs and chloroplast, but the CQDs does act as a light converter, which explains the lower fluorescence intensity peak of the CQDs/chloroplast complex when compared to the chloroplast alone [39]. The result supported that chlorophyll reabsorbs CQDs's blue FL emission for photosynthesis in chloroplasts, despite the overlap of CQDs's FL emission and chlorophyll absorbance spectra [14,21].

This study demonstrates the significant potential of CQDs as photosynthesis enhancers in indoor hydroponic systems, particularly for green mustard cultivation. Through comprehensive analysis of various CQDs concentrations, we identified 150 ppm as the optimal concentration for enhancing plant growth and photosynthetic efficiency. At this concentration, CQDs significantly improved key growth parameters, including biomass accumulation, leaf area development, and plant height, while simultaneously enhancing photosynthetic performance through increased CO2 assimilation and Rubisco activity. A notable finding was the differential behaviour of CQDs under varying light conditions. Under white light, CQDs primarily functioned as light converters, effectively transforming UV radiation into PAR. In contrast, under R/B light, CQDs acted as photosensitizers, enhancing energy transfer efficiency. The superior performance observed under white light suggests that broad-spectrum illumination, combined with CQDs treatment, provides optimal conditions for indoor hydroponic cultivation. The electron transfer mechanism studies revealed that CQDs-treated plants exhibited enhanced photochemical efficiency, particularly under white light conditions. This was evidenced by improved Fv/Fm values and higher quantum yield of PSII (ΦPSII), indicating more efficient light energy conversion and utilization. The increased rubisco activity and improved intrinsic water use efficiency (iWUE) at 150 ppm further support the role of CQDs in optimizing photosynthetic processes. These findings have significant implications for sustainable agriculture, particularly in indoor farming systems. The application of CQDs represents a promising approach to enhance crop productivity while maintaining resource efficiency. This study contributes valuable insights to the growing field of nano-agriculture, demonstrating how CQDs can be effectively utilized to enhance plant growth and development in controlled environment agriculture. Future research involving advanced imaging techniques, such as confocal microscopy with fluorescently tagged CQDs, is necessary to confirm the localization or deposition of CQDs on the chloroplast surface. This will provide deeper mechanistic insight into how CQDs enhance photosynthetic efficiency at the cellular level.

Yamuna A.P. Chowmasundaram: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Tong Ling Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Nadia Izati Fadzil: Writing – review & editing. Rosimah Nulit: Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis. Mashitah Jusoh: Validation, Supervision. Suraya Abdul Rashid: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
